congenital hepatic fibrosis with portal hypertension and esophageal varices,
sepsis and evidence of shock with end organ ischemia
respiratory failure given overhwhelming metabolic acidosis and inability to maintain respiratory compensation
Initially continued on broad spectrum antibiotics with Vancomycin and Piperacillin/Tazobactam
Antimicrobial coverage was initially broadened with doxycylcine as well as IV metronidazole,
GNR's were growing in her blood stream
cultures were growing Klebsiella Pneumoniae resistant to piperacillin/tazobactam
Lactate levels continued to increase, at one point >6
antimicrobial therapy was weaned to just meropenem, then switched to ceftriaxone once in house sensitivities came back.
abdominal CT scan showed possible right sided colitis
tagged WBC scan was pursued which was negative.
renal function improved over the next several days.
On transfer to the general medicine floor
overwhelming acidosis and inability to maintain respiratory compensation.
fell into multiple bouts of SVT to 180's which broke
Empiric heparin was started for 48 hours then discontinued.
TTE was performed which confirmed this, with a new EF of 40%
beta blockers were initiated as well as a statin
continued on her atorvastatin, metoprolol, and aspirin
no need for urgent cath at this time and she should follow up with an outpatient stress test
Acute kidney injury likely a result of pre renal failure progressiving to acute tubular necrosis from hypotension
creatinine continued to improve to baseline values
thrombocytopenic with accompanying anemia initially concerning for DIC
no schistocytes evidence on peripheral smears
the patient's crit and platelets were trended.
Fluid water deficit was calculated to >4.5 liters.
Free water boluses were started with her tube feeds, and IV D5W was started
No evidence of diabetes insipidus was seen on urine studies.
Evidence of flattened a-line with blood clot seen on ultrasound
thrombus and also evidence of distal ischemia on fingers/toes
adequately treat thrombus along with STEMI
non-occlusive basillic and occlusive cephalic vein thrombus, after developing R arm swelling
ok to continue using the PICC
as long as the patient does not develop any new R arm swelling, tenderness, or pain
She will be discharged with PICC
She will need the PICC line removed

metoprolol and lisinopril need to be titrated up as necessary.
